ground_truth.title: Thoughts on Zig
ground_truth.author: Sam Bossley
ground_truth.main_content:
Zig has the syntax of Rust, the runtime safety of Rust, the error handling of Go, the toolchain of Go, the module importing of JavaScript, the performance of C, the build system of Nix, what's not to like? Lately I've been spending some free time playing around with the Zig programming language and it's now one of my favorite programming languages. It borrows the best features from all of my favorite languages. Because of this, I want to take some time to give my review of the Zig language - the good, the bad, and the ugly - in hopes that you try it out yourself.

The Good: Language Design - Zig's language design is absolutely phenomenal. The syntax is explicit while still remaining concise. In Zig, errors are values that can be passed around and typed in function signatures. Zig also supports using some blocks as expressions, including conditionals, exhaustive switches, and labeled blocks. Zig has done a great job reducing ambiguity with no function overloads or hidden parameters. All of these language features make Zig code easy to read without being a Zig expert.

Fine-Tuned Memory Management - Rather than hide memory management like in Rust or Go, Zig emphasizes manual memory management by providing developers with the necessary tools like allocators and defer to prevent leaks. Zig employs precise variable bit widths for structs, integers, and other types, making it incredibly efficient.

Native C/C++ Interop - Zig has built-in C and C++ interoperability. This opens the door for Zig projects to include C libraries. I've always wanted to use C libraries for small side projects but strongly dislike the C language. Zig is now the perfect substitute.

Built-in Inline Testing - Zig supports inline testing like Rust, meaning you don't have to export functions and data structures just to test them. Inline testing collocates code with tests, reducing cognitive overhead.

Comptime - Probably the most interesting feature of Zig is its comptime feature, allowing developers to evaluate expressions and blocks at compile time if they choose. One of the coolest uses of comptime I've seen is ensuring no errors exist in subsequent code at compile time.

Build System - Zig supports a declarative build system that standardizes building other Zig projects using a common CLI interface so you don't have to install Meson, GNU Make, Ninja, Autoconf, etc. It just works.

The Bad: Frequent Breaking Changes - Because Zig is still in an alpha development state, it contains frequent breaking changes between releases. I can't in good conscience place blame on the language because I appreciate the effort to perfect the language, but I wish the breaking changes were announced and emphasized clearly.

The Ugly: Poor Documentation - Unfortunately, Zig's Achilles heel is its documentation. The Zig documentation is simultaneously too verbose and not verbose enough. In many cases, the documentation simply doesn't exist. There's no description of what certain functions do. In contrast, some documentation pages like std.log have fantastic documentation.

Other Notes: The only feature I haven't really worked with is Zig's dependency system. I don't completely understand how Zig's zon files work.

Conclusion: Zig is now one of my favorite programming languages and I love the direction it is headed. Although it has lacking documentation, I am optimistic in its bright future and happily anticipate Zig's first stable release. You can expect more Zig side projects from me in the future.

Rather than hide memory management like in Rust or Go, Zig instead emphasizes manual memory management by providing developers with the necessary tools (allocators, defer) to prevent leaks.
Zig is now one of my favorite programming languages and I love the direction it is headed. Although it has lacking documentation, I am optimistic in its bright future.
